response:
to store the value of the factorial, we must first understand how many factors of ten are contained within one hundred factorial.
***
we need to store the count of prime factors five and two to determine the power of ten that divides the number completely.
***
the number of factors of five in one hundred factorial is found by dividing one hundred by five and summing the quotients.
***
after we store the total count of fives, we see there are twenty-five factors of five in the entire product.
***
since there are more factors of two than five, the number of trailing zeros is determined by the twenty-five factors of five.
***
we must store the remaining power of ten after removing the twenty-five zeros that naturally appear at the end.
***
subtracting twenty-five from fifty leaves us with twenty-five powers of ten that remain in the denominator of our fraction.
***
to store the final result correctly, we recognize that the numerator will not be divisible by any more factors of five.
***
the denominator of the irreducible fraction is therefore ten raised to the power of twenty-five.
***
we have successfully stored the answer, which is ten to the twenty-fifth power, as the final denominator.
